words.definition: the usual order of things you do every day
example_sentences: I have a morning routine that never changes.
She follows the same routine every weekday.
It helps to have a consistent routine.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I do my routine' — say 'I follow/stick to my routine'.

words.definition: to travel regularly between home and work
example_sentences: She commutes to work by train every day.
My commute takes about an hour each way.
He hates the long commute in rush hour.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I make a commute' — say 'I commute' or 'my commute takes...'.

words.definition: a plan showing what will happen and when
example_sentences: I have a very busy schedule this week.
Can we schedule a meeting for Thursday?
The project is on schedule.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have a full programme' — say 'I have a busy schedule'.

words.definition: an arranged time to meet someone professionally
example_sentences: I have a doctor's appointment at 3pm.
She made an appointment with the hairdresser.
Don't be late for your appointment.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have a meeting with the doctor' — say 'I have an appointment'.

words.definition: something you do regularly, often without thinking
example_sentences: Checking my phone first thing is a bad habit.
She has a habit of arriving early everywhere.
Regular exercise is a healthy habit to develop.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have the habit to check' — say 'I have a habit of checking'.

words.definition: a routine household task that must be done
example_sentences: Doing the dishes is my least favourite chore.
We share the household chores equally.
He hates doing chores at the weekend.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I do works at home' — say 'I do chores' or 'household chores'.

words.definition: a short trip to do a specific task such as buying or collecting something
example_sentences: I have a few errands to run this afternoon.
Can you run a quick errand for me?
She spent the morning running errands around town.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I go to do some tasks' — say 'I have errands to run'.

words.definition: the latest time or date by which something must be completed
example_sentences: The deadline for the report is Friday morning.
She always works best under deadline pressure.
They missed the deadline by two days.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have a last date' — say 'I have a deadline'.

words.definition: the thing that is most important and should be done first
example_sentences: Family is my absolute top priority.
You need to get your priorities in order.
Exercise has become a daily priority for her.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'My first importance is' — say 'My top priority is'.

words.definition: to relax and reduce stress after a busy period
example_sentences: I like to unwind with a good book in the evenings.
After a long week, I desperately need to unwind.
A walk in the park always helps me unwind.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I relax myself' — say 'I unwind' or simply 'I relax'.
words.collocations: ['unwind after work', 'need to unwind', 'help you unwind']

words.definition: arriving or doing things at exactly the right time
example_sentences: She is always punctual — I've never seen her late.
It's very important to be punctual for job interviews.
Being punctual shows respect for other people's time.
words.pronunciation_tip: PUNK-choo-ul — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'She is always on time' then 'punctual' — just use one.

words.definition: to give or set aside time, money, or resources for a purpose
example_sentences: I allocate two hours a day to exercise.
The manager allocates tasks to each team member.
You should allocate more time to preparation.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I give time to exercise' — say 'I allocate time to exercise'.

words.definition: feeling that you have too much to deal with at once
example_sentences: I'm completely overwhelmed by my workload right now.
She felt overwhelmed by the amount of information.
Don't get overwhelmed — take it one step at a time.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I have too much work' only — say 'I feel overwhelmed by my workload'.

words.definition: to manage several tasks or responsibilities at the same time
example_sentences: She juggles work and family life brilliantly.
It's very hard to juggle multiple projects simultaneously.
He is juggling three jobs at the moment.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'She does many things at the same time' — say 'She juggles work and family'.

words.definition: to keep delaying something you should do
example_sentences: He tends to procrastinate when he faces difficult tasks.
Stop procrastinating and just start writing!
She procrastinated all week and then rushed at the end.
words.pronunciation_tip: proh-KRAS-tih-nayt — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I delay my work' — say 'I procrastinate' or 'I put things off'.

words.definition: able to be maintained for a long time without causing harm
example_sentences: Is your current work-life balance sustainable?
We need a more sustainable approach to managing our time.
Working 80 hours a week is simply not sustainable.
words.pronunciation_tip: sus-TAYN-uh-bul — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'This can continue' — say 'This is sustainable'.

words.definition: to do something you missed or did not have time to do
example_sentences: I need to catch up on my emails after the holiday.
Let's meet for coffee and catch up properly.
She caught up on her sleep over the long weekend.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I need to do the things I missed' — say 'I need to catch up on...'
words.collocations: ['catch up on emails', 'catch up with someone', 'need to catch up']

words.definition: to save or reserve time or money for a specific purpose
example_sentences: I set aside an hour every morning for reading.
You should set aside some money for unexpected costs.
She sets aside time for exercise every single day.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I keep time for reading' — say 'I set aside time for reading'.
words.collocations: ['set aside time', 'set aside money', 'set aside for']

words.definition: to use all of something so none is left
example_sentences: I've run out of time to finish the project.
We've completely run out of milk again.
She ran out of patience with the constant delays.
words.pronunciation_tip: run OUT of — stress on out.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I finished the time' — say 'I've run out of time'.
words.collocations: ['run out of time', 'run out of money', 'run out of patience']

words.definition: to continue at the same level or pace as someone or something
example_sentences: It's very hard to keep up with all the latest news.
She struggles to keep up with the fast pace of work.
He finds it difficult to keep up with his more experienced colleagues.
words.pronunciation_tip: keep UP with — stress on up.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I can't follow the news' — say 'I can't keep up with the news'.
words.collocations: ['keep up with news', 'keep up with work', 'hard to keep up with']
